1:
"Follow after charity, and desire spiritual gifts, but rather that ye may prophesy." The witnesses note the call to pursue love and desire prophecy above tongues. On "follow after charity," the grammatical voices note the priority. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, having exalted love in ch. 13, now bids them "follow after" (pursue earnestly, as in a chase) charity (love) — making it the first object of pursuit, the atmosphere of all gift-use.

On "desire spiritual gifts, but rather that ye may prophesy," the witnesses note the order. Barnes and Calvin note they may rightly desire spiritual gifts (charismata), but especially prophesying — the gift of speaking edifyingly under inspiration in the known tongue — which Paul will show is superior to tongues for the church's good.

The eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the balance: love is to be pursued above all, and among gifts prophecy preferred; the chapter's whole aim is to subordinate the showy gift of tongues to the edifying gift of prophecy, all governed by love. The verse displays the call to pursue love and desire prophecy.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul bids them "follow after" (pursue earnestly) charity, making love the first object (Barnes, Robertson); they may desire spiritual gifts, but especially prophesying — the gift of edifying inspired speech in the known tongue, superior to tongues for the church's good (Barnes, Calvin). Chrysostom and Henry note love is pursued above all, prophecy preferred among gifts, the chapter subordinating tongues to prophecy under love. The verse displays the call to love and prophecy. The verse marks the pursuit: follow after charity. The verse displays the desire: spiritual gifts. The verse displays the preference: that ye prophesy. The verse displays the aim: edification under love. The call displays love and prophecy set above tongues. The verse displays the charity: pursued. The verse displays the prophecy: preferred. The verse displays the order: love, then gifts. The call to pursue love and desire prophecy above tongues displays the governing principle of the chapter: love is to be earnestly followed, and among the gifts prophecy — edifying inspired speech — is to be preferred for the building up of the church. The verse opens the comparison of prophecy and tongues, grounded in edification (vv.2-5).
2:
"For he that speaketh in an unknown tongue speaketh not unto men, but unto God: for no man understandeth him; howbeit in the spirit he speaketh mysteries." The witnesses note that one speaking in a tongue is not understood by men. On "speaketh in an unknown tongue," the grammatical voices note the gift. Barnes and Robertson note "tongue" denotes the power of speaking a foreign language (as at Pentecost, Acts 2) — "unknown" supplied because unknown to the hearers; the gift exercised without interpretation profits the assembly nothing.

On "speaketh not unto men, but unto God: no man understandeth," the witnesses note the uselessness to hearers. Barnes notes that since no one present understands the language, the speaker addresses God (who understands all) rather than men, who derive no instruction.

On "in the spirit he speaketh mysteries," the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the inward exercise: by the Spirit (or in his own spirit) he utters mysteries — truths hidden from the hearers because unintelligible to them; the gift, though real, is unfruitful without interpretation. The verse displays that one speaking in a tongue is not understood by men.

The witnesses agree: "tongue" denotes the power of speaking a foreign language (as at Pentecost), "unknown" supplied because unknown to the hearers; the gift without interpretation profiting the assembly nothing (Barnes, Robertson); since none present understands, the speaker addresses God rather than men, who derive no instruction (Barnes); and "in the spirit he speaketh mysteries" — truths hidden from the hearers because unintelligible, the gift real but unfruitful without interpretation (Calvin, Wesley). The verse displays the tongue not understood. The verse marks the gift: a foreign tongue. The verse displays the hearers: understand not. The verse displays the address: to God. The verse displays the mysteries: hidden, unfruitful. The tongue displays the gift useless to the assembly without interpretation. The verse displays the language: foreign. The verse displays the speaking: to God. The verse displays the mysteries: unintelligible. That one speaking in a tongue is not understood by men displays the inherent limitation of the gift in public worship: speaking a foreign language none present understands, he addresses God and utters mysteries hidden from the hearers — edifying no one but himself unless interpreted. The verse displays the unprofitableness of tongues alone, set against prophecy (v.3).
3:
"But he that prophesieth speaketh unto men to edification, and exhortation, and comfort." The brief verse notes that prophecy edifies, exhorts, and comforts. The grammatical voices note the three effects. Barnes and Robertson note that, by contrast with tongues, the prophet (one who speaks intelligibly under inspiration) "speaketh unto men" — to their profit, in three ways: edification (building up in faith), exhortation (stirring to duty), and comfort (consolation in trial).

On "to edification, exhortation, comfort," the witnesses note the church's benefit. Barnes notes these are the very things the assembly needs and that tongues (unintelligible) cannot give; prophecy meets the hearers' spiritual needs.

The Reformed Calvin notes the superiority: because prophecy is understood, it actually builds, rouses, and consoles the church — proving it the greater gift for public worship. The verse displays that prophecy edifies, exhorts, and comforts.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: by contrast with tongues, the prophet (one who speaks intelligibly under inspiration) "speaketh unto men" to their profit — in edification (building up), exhortation (stirring to duty), and comfort (consolation, Barnes, Robertson); these the very things the assembly needs and tongues cannot give (Barnes). Calvin notes that because prophecy is understood, it actually builds, rouses, and consoles, proving it the greater gift. The verse displays prophecy's threefold benefit. The verse marks the address: unto men. The verse displays the effects: edification, exhortation, comfort. The verse displays the contrast: with tongues. The verse displays the superiority: the greater gift. Prophecy displays the threefold profit of intelligible inspired speech. The verse displays the edifying: building up. The verse displays the exhortation: stirring. The verse displays the comfort: consoling. That prophecy edifies, exhorts, and comforts displays the threefold profit of intelligible inspired speech: addressing men in a known tongue, the prophet builds up their faith, stirs them to duty, and consoles them in trial — meeting the very needs the assembly has and tongues cannot supply. The verse displays the superiority of prophecy for the church, sharpened in the comparison (v.4). These three words together sketch the whole pastoral work of the gospel ministry, building up the weak in their most holy faith, rousing the slothful to obedience, and pouring consolation into the wounded heart; and it is precisely because the unintelligible tongue can do none of these that the apostle sets prophecy so decidedly above it.
4:
"He that speaketh in an unknown tongue edifieth himself; but he that prophesieth edifieth the church." The brief verse notes that prophecy edifies the church, tongues only oneself. The grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the decisive comparison: the tongue-speaker "edifieth himself" only — he may be inwardly moved and benefited, but the assembly gains nothing; the prophet "edifieth the church" — the whole body is built up.

On "edifieth himself," the witnesses note the limit. Barnes notes the tongue-speaker derives private benefit (his own devotion is stirred), but in public worship the aim is the edification of all, which tongues alone do not serve.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the principle: the worth of a public gift is measured by its usefulness to the church; prophecy, edifying all, is therefore preferable to tongues, which edify only the speaker. The verse displays that prophecy edifies the church, tongues only oneself.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the decisive comparison — the tongue-speaker "edifieth himself" only, inwardly moved but the assembly gaining nothing, while the prophet "edifieth the church," the whole body built up (Barnes, Robertson); the tongue-speaker deriving private benefit but public worship aiming at the edification of all (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note the worth of a public gift is measured by its usefulness to the church, so prophecy is preferable. The verse displays the contrast of edification. The verse marks the tongue: edifies himself. The verse displays the prophecy: edifies the church. The verse displays the limit: private benefit only. The verse displays the principle: usefulness to all. The contrast displays prophecy's superior public worth. The verse displays the self: edified by tongues. The verse displays the church: edified by prophecy. The verse displays the measure: usefulness. That prophecy edifies the church while tongues edify only oneself displays the principle that governs public worship: a gift's worth is measured by its usefulness to all, so the tongue, benefiting only the speaker, yields to prophecy, which builds up the whole church. The verse displays the decisive ground of prophecy's preference, qualified by Paul's wish (v.5).
5:
"I would that ye all spake with tongues, but rather that ye prophesied: for greater is he that prophesieth than he that speaketh with tongues, except he interpret, that the church may receive edifying." The witnesses note prophecy preferred to tongues, unless tongues be interpreted. On "I would that ye all spake with tongues, but rather that ye prophesied," the grammatical voices note the wish. Barnes and Robertson note Paul does not despise tongues — he would be glad if all had the gift — but he prefers that they prophesy, the more useful gift.

On "greater is he that prophesieth... except he interpret," the witnesses note the qualification. Barnes and Calvin note the prophet is "greater" (more useful in the church) than the tongue-speaker — unless the tongue-speaker also interprets, so that the church may understand and be edified; interpretation puts tongues on a level with prophecy for usefulness.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the standard: edification ("that the church may receive edifying") is the measure throughout; whatever builds up the church is to be preferred. The verse displays prophecy preferred to tongues, unless interpreted.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul does not despise tongues — he would be glad if all had the gift — but prefers that they prophesy, the more useful gift (Barnes, Robertson); the prophet being "greater" (more useful) than the tongue-speaker, unless the latter also interprets so the church may be edified, interpretation putting tongues on a level for usefulness (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note edification is the measure throughout. The verse displays prophecy preferred unless tongues interpreted. The verse marks the wish: all spake with tongues. The verse displays the preference: rather prophesy. The verse displays the qualification: except he interpret. The verse displays the standard: edification. The preference displays edification as the measure of the gifts. The verse displays the tongues: not despised. The verse displays the prophecy: preferred. The verse displays the interpretation: putting tongues on a level. Prophecy preferred to tongues, unless tongues be interpreted, displays the consistent standard of edification: Paul values tongues but prefers prophecy as more useful, granting that interpreted tongues serve the church equally — whatever builds up the church being preferred. The verse displays the rule of usefulness governing the gifts, illustrated by Paul's own coming (v.6).
6:
"Now, brethren, if I come unto you speaking with tongues, what shall I profit you, except I shall speak to you either by revelation, or by knowledge, or by prophesying, or by doctrine?" The witnesses note that unintelligible tongues profit nothing without intelligible teaching. On "if I come speaking with tongues, what shall I profit you?" the grammatical voices note the example. Barnes and Robertson note Paul applies the principle to himself: if even he came to them speaking only in tongues, he would profit them nothing — unless he also spoke intelligibly.

On "by revelation, knowledge, prophesying, or doctrine," the witnesses note the useful forms. Barnes and Henry note the four intelligible modes that do profit: revelation (a truth disclosed), knowledge (understanding imparted), prophesying (inspired edifying speech), or doctrine (teaching) — all conveyed so as to be understood.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: the gift is worthless to hearers unless it conveys understanding; even an apostle's tongues, uninterpreted, would not edify. The verse displays that unintelligible tongues profit nothing without intelligible teaching.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul applies the principle to himself — if even he came speaking only in tongues, he would profit them nothing unless he also spoke intelligibly (Barnes, Robertson); the four intelligible modes that profit being revelation (a truth disclosed), knowledge (understanding imparted), prophesying (edifying speech), and doctrine (teaching), all conveyed to be understood (Barnes, Henry). Calvin notes the gift is worthless to hearers unless it conveys understanding. The verse displays tongues profitless without teaching. The verse marks the example: Paul's own coming. The verse displays the question: what profit? The verse displays the useful forms: revelation, knowledge, prophesying, doctrine. The verse displays the principle: understanding required. The example displays even Paul's tongues unprofitable without sense. The verse displays the tongues: alone profitless. The verse displays the forms: intelligible. The verse displays the profit: by understanding. That unintelligible tongues profit nothing without intelligible teaching displays the principle by Paul's own example: were he to come speaking only in tongues, he would benefit them nothing, unless by revelation, knowledge, prophesying, or doctrine he conveyed understanding. The verse displays the necessity of intelligibility, illustrated from lifeless instruments (vv.7-9).
7:
"And even things without life giving sound, whether pipe or harp, except they give a distinction in the sounds, how shall it be known what is piped or harped?" The brief verse notes the analogy of lifeless instruments giving distinct sounds. The grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note Paul argues from lifeless instruments (the pipe/flute and harp): even they must give "a distinction in the sounds" — distinct, recognizable notes — or no one can tell what tune is played.

On "how shall it be known," the witnesses note the point. Barnes notes that if even instruments must produce intelligible, distinguishable sounds to convey a tune, how much more must human speech be intelligible to convey meaning.

The Reformed Calvin notes the force: confused, indistinct sounds (whether of instruments or of tongues) convey nothing; clarity is essential to communication. The verse displays the analogy of lifeless instruments giving distinct sounds.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul argues from lifeless instruments (pipe and harp) — even they must give "a distinction in the sounds," distinct recognizable notes, or no one can tell what tune is played (Barnes, Robertson); if even instruments must produce distinguishable sounds to convey a tune, how much more must human speech be intelligible (Barnes). Calvin notes confused sounds, of instruments or tongues, convey nothing; clarity is essential. The verse displays the analogy of instruments. The verse marks the figure: pipe and harp. The verse displays the requirement: distinct sounds. The verse displays the point: clarity for meaning. The verse displays the inference: how much more speech. The analogy displays the need of distinct sounds to convey meaning. The verse displays the instruments: lifeless. The verse displays the distinction: in sounds. The verse displays the lesson: clarity. The analogy of lifeless instruments giving distinct sounds displays the necessity of intelligibility: as even a pipe or harp must sound distinct notes for the tune to be known, so human speech must be clear to convey meaning. The verse displays the argument from music for intelligible speech, extended to the trumpet (v.8).
8:
"For if the trumpet give an uncertain sound, who shall prepare himself to the battle?" The brief verse notes the analogy of the trumpet's uncertain sound. The grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note a second musical illustration: the war-trumpet, whose distinct calls signal the army; if it gives "an uncertain sound" (indistinct, ambiguous), no soldier can know whether to prepare for battle, advance, or retreat.

On "who shall prepare himself," the witnesses note the consequence. Barnes notes the practical point: an unclear signal produces confusion and unreadiness; clarity is essential where action depends on the message.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the application: as an indistinct trumpet fails its purpose, so unintelligible tongues fail to rouse or instruct the church; the message must be clear to have effect. The verse displays the analogy of the trumpet's uncertain sound.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the war-trumpet, whose distinct calls signal the army, if it gives "an uncertain sound" (indistinct, ambiguous), leaves no soldier able to know whether to prepare for battle (Barnes, Robertson); an unclear signal producing confusion and unreadiness, clarity being essential where action depends on the message (Barnes). Wesley notes that as an indistinct trumpet fails its purpose, so unintelligible tongues fail to rouse the church. The verse displays the trumpet analogy. The verse marks the figure: the trumpet. The verse displays the fault: an uncertain sound. The verse displays the consequence: no one prepares. The verse displays the application: tongues fail to rouse. The analogy displays clarity essential to effective signal. The verse displays the trumpet: indistinct. The verse displays the soldier: unprepared. The verse displays the lesson: clarity needed. The analogy of the trumpet's uncertain sound displays the necessity of a clear message: as an indistinct trumpet leaves the soldier unprepared for battle, so unintelligible tongues fail to instruct or rouse the church. The verse displays the second musical argument for clarity, applied directly to their speech (v.9). The figure was a peculiarly vivid one in an age of constant warfare, when whole armies moved and halted at the bidding of the trumpet, and a single confused blast might throw a host into ruin; so too, the apostle would have them see, a single hour of unintelligible speech might leave a whole congregation unstirred and unfed.
9:
"So likewise ye, except ye utter by the tongue words easy to be understood, how shall it be known what is spoken? for ye shall speak into the air." The brief verse applies the analogy: speak intelligible words or speak into the air. The grammatical voices note the application. Barnes and Robertson note Paul applies the musical illustrations: "so likewise ye" — unless they utter "words easy to be understood" (intelligible, plain), no one will know what is spoken, and they speak in vain.

On "ye shall speak into the air," the witnesses note the futility. Barnes notes the vivid image: unintelligible speech is "into the air" — wasted, reaching no understanding, accomplishing nothing.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the lesson: the whole point of speech is to convey meaning; tongues without interpretation, however impressive, are empty sound. The verse displays the application: speak intelligible words or speak into the air.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul applies the musical illustrations — "so likewise ye," unless they utter "words easy to be understood" (intelligible, plain), no one will know what is spoken, and they speak in vain (Barnes, Robertson); "into the air" a vivid image of unintelligible speech wasted, reaching no understanding (Barnes). Wesley notes the whole point of speech is to convey meaning; tongues without interpretation are empty sound. The verse displays the application. The verse marks the rule: words easy to be understood. The verse displays the failure: how shall it be known? The verse displays the futility: into the air. The verse displays the lesson: speech conveys meaning. The application displays unintelligible speech wasted. The verse displays the words: must be plain. The verse displays the speaking: into the air. The verse displays the futility: empty sound. The application — speak intelligible words or speak into the air — displays the futility of unintelligible speech: unless they utter plain words that can be understood, no one knows what is spoken, and they merely speak into the air. The verse displays the vanity of tongues without sense, grounded in the meaningfulness of all languages (vv.10-11).
10:
"There are, it may be, so many kinds of voices in the world, and none of them is without signification." The brief verse notes that every language has meaning. The grammatical voices note the observation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul observes that there are many kinds of "voices" (languages, tongues) in the world, and none is "without signification" — each language conveys real meaning to those who know it.

On the inference, the witnesses note the implication. Barnes notes that since every language is meaningful (to its own speakers), a language is useful only where it is understood; meaning depends on the hearer's comprehension.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: languages are not empty sounds but vehicles of meaning — yet meaning unshared is useless; the gift of tongues, to profit, requires that the meaning be conveyed. The verse displays that every language has meaning.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul observes there are many kinds of "voices" (languages) in the world, and none is "without signification" — each conveying real meaning to those who know it (Barnes, Robertson); since every language is meaningful to its own speakers, it is useful only where understood, meaning depending on the hearer's comprehension (Barnes). Calvin notes languages are vehicles of meaning, yet meaning unshared is useless. The verse displays every language meaningful. The verse marks the observation: many voices. The verse displays the truth: none without meaning. The verse displays the inference: useful only where understood. The verse displays the point: meaning must be shared. The observation displays every tongue meaningful to its own speakers. The verse displays the voices: many. The verse displays the signification: real. The verse displays the condition: understanding. That every language has meaning displays the truth underlying Paul's argument: all the world's languages convey real signification to those who know them — so a tongue profits only where it is understood, meaning being useless unless shared. The verse displays the dependence of meaning on comprehension, drawn out in the figure of the barbarian (v.11). The very abundance and variety of the world languages, each perfect and expressive in its own place, only serves to underline the apostle point, that a language has worth not in itself but in being understood, and that what edifies in one assembly is mere noise in another where its meaning is unknown.
11:
"Therefore if I know not the meaning of the voice, I shall be unto him that speaketh a barbarian, and he that speaketh shall be a barbarian unto me." The brief verse notes that unintelligible speech makes speaker and hearer foreigners to each other. The grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note Paul draws the inference: if he does not know the meaning of a language ("the voice"), then he and the speaker are mutual "barbarians" — the Greek term for a foreigner whose speech is unintelligible (an echo of unintelligible sound).

On the mutual estrangement, the witnesses note the point. Barnes notes that without shared understanding, the speaker and hearer are as strangers to one another — communication fails entirely, each foreign to the other.

The Reformed Calvin notes the application: so it is in the church when tongues are used without interpretation — speaker and hearers are mutually estranged, the assembly profiting nothing. The verse displays that unintelligible speech makes speaker and hearer foreigners.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: if Paul does not know the meaning of a language, then he and the speaker are mutual "barbarians" — the Greek term for a foreigner whose speech is unintelligible (Barnes, Robertson); without shared understanding, speaker and hearer are as strangers, communication failing entirely (Barnes). Calvin notes so it is in the church when tongues are used without interpretation — speaker and hearers mutually estranged. The verse displays speaker and hearer made foreigners. The verse marks the figure: a barbarian. The verse displays the condition: knowing not the meaning. The verse displays the estrangement: mutual. The verse displays the application: to the church. The figure displays unintelligible speech estranging speaker and hearer. The verse displays the not-knowing: of the voice. The verse displays the barbarian: each to the other. The verse displays the failure: of communication. That unintelligible speech makes speaker and hearer foreigners to each other displays the complete failure of communication without understanding: not knowing the language, each is a "barbarian" to the other, the assembly profiting nothing. The verse displays the mutual estrangement of unintelligible tongues, leading to the rule of seeking edification (v.12).
12:
"Even so ye, forasmuch as ye are zealous of spiritual gifts, seek that ye may excel to the edifying of the church." The brief verse notes the rule: seek gifts that edify the church. The grammatical voices note the exhortation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul turns their very zeal to good account: "forasmuch as ye are zealous" (eager, ambitious) for spiritual gifts, let them direct that zeal rightly — to "excel to the edifying of the church," that is, to abound in gifts useful for building up the body.

On "to the edifying of the church," the witnesses note the standard. Barnes notes the controlling principle: edification of the church is the measure by which gifts are to be valued and sought; their ambition should be for what profits all.

The Reformed Calvin notes the redirection: Paul does not quench their zeal but channels it — seek to abound, but in the gifts that build up the church, not in the showy gift of tongues. The verse displays the rule: seek gifts that edify the church.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul turns their zeal to good account — "forasmuch as ye are zealous" for spiritual gifts, let them direct that zeal to "excel to the edifying of the church," abounding in gifts useful for building up the body (Barnes, Robertson); edification being the measure by which gifts are valued and sought (Barnes). Calvin notes Paul does not quench their zeal but channels it toward gifts that build up, not the showy gift of tongues. The verse displays the rule of edifying gifts. The verse marks the zeal: for spiritual gifts. The verse displays the direction: excel to edifying. The verse displays the standard: the church's building up. The verse displays the redirection: channelled zeal. The rule displays edification as the goal of holy ambition. The verse displays the zeal: redirected. The verse displays the excelling: in useful gifts. The verse displays the church: edified. The rule to seek gifts that edify the church displays the right channel for their zeal: eager for spiritual gifts, they are to aim at abounding in those that build up the body — edification being the measure of all holy ambition for gifts. The verse displays the redirection of their zeal toward usefulness, applied to the praying of the tongue-speaker (v.13).
13:
"Wherefore let him that speaketh in an unknown tongue pray that he may interpret." The brief verse notes the rule that the tongue-speaker pray for interpretation. The grammatical voices note the direction. Barnes and Robertson note the practical instruction: one who has the gift of tongues should "pray that he may interpret" — seek (by prayer) the further gift of interpretation, so that what he speaks may be made intelligible and edify the church.

On the purpose, the witnesses note the aim. Barnes notes the goal is edification: tongues become useful when interpreted; so the tongue-speaker should desire and pray for the power to render his speech understandable.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: the gift of tongues, to be of any public use, must be joined to interpretation; hence the speaker is to seek that gift, that his tongue may serve the church. The verse displays the rule that the tongue-speaker pray for interpretation.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: one who has the gift of tongues should "pray that he may interpret" — seek by prayer the further gift of interpretation, so that what he speaks may be intelligible and edify the church (Barnes, Robertson); the goal being edification, tongues becoming useful when interpreted (Barnes). Calvin notes the gift of tongues, to be of public use, must be joined to interpretation, which the speaker is to seek. The verse displays the rule of praying for interpretation. The verse marks the direction: pray that he may interpret. The verse displays the aim: intelligible speech. The verse displays the purpose: edification. The verse displays the principle: tongues joined to interpretation. The rule displays interpretation sought to make tongues useful. The verse displays the speaker: praying. The verse displays the interpretation: sought. The verse displays the edification: the goal. The rule that the tongue-speaker pray for interpretation displays the way to make the gift useful: he is to seek by prayer the power to interpret, so that his tongue may be made intelligible and serve the edification of the church. The verse displays the joining of tongues to interpretation, explained by the unfruitfulness of the understanding (vv.14-15).
14:
"For if I pray in an unknown tongue, my spirit prayeth, but my understanding is unfruitful." The witnesses note the unfruitfulness of the understanding when praying in a tongue. On "if I pray in an unknown tongue, my spirit prayeth," the grammatical voices note the partial benefit. Barnes and Robertson note that if one prays in a tongue, "my spirit prayeth" — his own inner being (or the Spirit's gift in him) is engaged and devout, but only privately.

On "my understanding is unfruitful," the witnesses note the deficiency. Barnes and Calvin note that the "understanding" (nous, the rational mind) is "unfruitful" — bears no fruit for others; either his own understanding produces nothing useful to the hearers, or (as some) his mind yields no benefit because the words are not understood. The prayer profits neither the assembly nor fully himself.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the lesson: true worship engages both the affections (spirit) and the understanding; prayer in a tongue, lacking the latter's fruit, is defective for public use. The verse displays the unfruitfulness of the understanding when praying in a tongue.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: if one prays in a tongue, "my spirit prayeth" — his inner being (or the Spirit's gift) is engaged and devout, but privately (Barnes, Robertson); while "my understanding is unfruitful," the rational mind bearing no fruit for others, the prayer profiting neither the assembly nor fully himself (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note true worship engages both affections and understanding; prayer in a tongue, lacking the latter's fruit, is defective for public use. The verse displays the understanding unfruitful. The verse marks the spirit: prayeth. The verse displays the understanding: unfruitful. The verse displays the deficiency: no fruit for others. The verse displays the lesson: worship needs both. The unfruitfulness displays the defect of tongue-prayer in public. The verse displays the spirit: engaged. The verse displays the mind: barren. The verse displays the prayer: unprofitable. The unfruitfulness of the understanding when praying in a tongue displays the defect of such prayer for the church: though the spirit is devoutly engaged, the understanding bears no fruit for others, so the prayer profits neither the assembly nor fully the one praying. The verse displays the need to engage the mind in worship, resolved in Paul's purpose (v.15).
15:
"What is it then? I will pray with the spirit, and I will pray with the understanding also: I will sing with the spirit, and I will sing with the understanding also." The witnesses note Paul's resolve to worship with both spirit and understanding. On "I will pray with the spirit, and with the understanding also," the grammatical voices note the resolution. Barnes and Robertson note Paul resolves the matter: he will pray "with the spirit" (with devout affection, or the Spirit's gift) and "with the understanding also" (intelligibly, so that the mind is engaged and others edified) — both together.

On "I will sing with the spirit, and with the understanding," the witnesses note the application to praise. Barnes and Henry note the same applies to singing: praise too is to engage both the heart's devotion and the intelligent understanding, in a known tongue.

The Reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the principle: acceptable public worship — whether prayer or praise — requires the union of fervent spirit and enlightened understanding; neither devotion without intelligence nor intelligence without devotion suffices. The verse displays Paul's resolve to worship with both spirit and understanding.

The witnesses agree: Paul resolves to pray "with the spirit" (devout affection, or the Spirit's gift) and "with the understanding also" (intelligibly, the mind engaged and others edified), both together (Barnes, Robertson); and the same for singing — praise too engaging both the heart's devotion and the intelligent understanding in a known tongue (Barnes, Henry). Calvin and Henry note acceptable public worship requires the union of fervent spirit and enlightened understanding. The verse displays worship with spirit and understanding. The verse marks the prayer: with both. The verse displays the singing: with both. The verse displays the union: devotion and intelligence. The verse displays the principle: acceptable worship. The resolve displays worship uniting fervent spirit and enlightened mind. The verse displays the spirit: engaged. The verse displays the understanding: engaged. The verse displays the union: in worship. Paul's resolve to worship with both spirit and understanding displays the model of acceptable public worship: prayer and praise alike are to unite devout affection with intelligent understanding, in a known tongue — neither fervour without sense nor sense without fervour. The verse displays the union of heart and mind in worship, applied to the unlearned hearer (vv.16-17).
16:
"Else when thou shalt bless with the spirit, how shall he that occupieth the room of the unlearned say Amen at thy giving of thanks, seeing he understandeth not what thou sayest?" The witnesses note that the unlearned cannot say Amen to an unintelligible thanksgiving. On "when thou shalt bless with the spirit," the grammatical voices note the case. 31:

32:

33:

34:
"Let your women keep silence in the churches: for it is not permitted unto them to speak; but they are commanded to be under obedience, as also saith the law." The witnesses note the command for women to keep silence in the churches — the women-silence debate. On "let your women keep silence in the churches," the grammatical voices note the rule. Barnes, Robertson, and Gill note Paul forbids women to "speak" in the public assembly — to teach, lead, or address the congregation authoritatively; the silence enjoined concerns public, authoritative speaking.

On the scope, the interpreters discuss the reach. Most (Chrysostom, Gill, Calvin, Henry) take it as a real prohibition of women teaching or speaking authoritatively in the assembly, grounded in the order of subordination. The difficulty is reconciling it with 11:5 (where women pray or prophesy): Henry and others hold that the prohibition is of authoritative public teaching and disorderly speaking, not contradicting the veiled praying of 11:5, or that 11:5 reproved only the manner; some limit v.34 to the disorderly asking of questions and the judging of prophecies.
